coredata.dc:title: In-situ anchoring Ag through organic polymer for configuring efficient plasmonic BiVO4 photoanode
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Peng
Bai, Hongye
Wang, Fagen
Yu, Hao
Xu, Dongbo
Fan, Weiqiang
Shi, Weidong
coredata.dc:description: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is a promising method for converting solar energy into hydrogen fuel, but how to further improve the efficiency of solar energy conversion is still a large challenge. Here, it is the first time to decorate BiVO4 with Ag and N-doped carbon (BiVO4-C/N-Ag) for efficient solar-to-chemical energy conversion, and we in-depth demonstrated the superiority of BiVO4-C/N-Ag heterostructure in PEC water splitting system. Polydopamine (PDA) as medium material realized the in-situ anchor of both Ag and N-doped carbon on BiVO4, which significantly boosted the charge injection efficiency (η inj) and charge separation efficiency (η sep) to 88.59% and 29.75% (1.23 V vs. RHE), respectively. The effective function of Ag and N-doped carbon exhibited an obvious influence on the enhancement of PEC performance. The photocurrent density of BiVO4-C/N-Ag increased up to 2.42 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, and 7.5-fold IPCE value (30%, 400 nm, 1.23 V vs. RHE) over bare BiVO4 has been achieved, indicating the successful configuration of a promising photoanode in the field of PEC water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4
N-doped carbon
Plasmonic photoanode
Photoelectrochemical water splitting

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag.
FE-SEM images for (a) BiVO4, (b) BiVO4-C/N, (c) BiVO4-C/N-Ag, and (d) cross-sectional image for BiVO4-C/N-Ag. (e) TEM and (f) HR-TEM of BiVO4-C/N-Ag.
XPS spectra of (a) Bi 4f, (b) V 2p, (c) O 1s, (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s, and (f) Ag 3d.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag. (b) Tauc plot of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag.

(a) The linear-sweep-voltammetry (LSV) under 100 mW/cm2 illumination (scan rate: 50 mV/s); (b) Transient photocurrent curves at 1.23 V vs. RHE under 100 mW/cm2 illumination (scan rate: 50 mV/s); (c) The photoelectrochemical impedance spectroscopy (PEIS) under illumination. Inset: the corresponding equivalent circuit. Rct is the charge transfer resistance across the electrode/electrolyte interface, Rs is the solution resistance, and CPE is the constant phase component; (d) IPCE spectra measured by monochromatic light of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag.

(a–c) Mott−Schottky plots measured at room temperature in the dark; (d) Charge injection efficiencies on the surface under 100 mW/cm2 illumination (H2O2 as a hole scavenger), and (e) Charge separation efficiencies in the bulk of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag. (f) Stability measurement of BiVO4-C/N-Ag under 100 mW/cm2 illumination.
Schematic mechanism of BiVO4-C/N-Ag photoanode for PEC water oxidation.
Synthetic route of the BiVO4-C/N-Ag photoanode.

head.ce:title: In-situ anchoring Ag through organic polymer for configuring efficient plasmonic BiVO4 photoanode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering
Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is a promising method for converting solar energy into hydrogen fuel, but how to further improve the efficiency of solar energy conversion is still a large challenge. Here, it is the first time to decorate BiVO4 with Ag and N-doped carbon (BiVO4-C/N-Ag) for efficient solar-to-chemical energy conversion, and we in-depth demonstrated the superiority of BiVO4-C/N-Ag heterostructure in PEC water splitting system. Polydopamine (PDA) as medium material realized the in-situ anchor of both Ag and N-doped carbon on BiVO4, which significantly boosted the charge injection efficiency (η inj) and charge separation efficiency (η sep) to 88.59% and 29.75% (1.23 V vs. RHE), respectively. The effective function of Ag and N-doped carbon exhibited an obvious influence on the enhancement of PEC performance. The photocurrent density of BiVO4-C/N-Ag increased up to 2.42 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, and 7.5-fold IPCE value (30%, 400 nm, 1.23 V vs. RHE) over bare BiVO4 has been achieved, indicating the successful configuration of a promising photoanode in the field of PEC water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: Toward serious energy and environmental crises, many efforts have been devoted to producing clean and efficient fuel in the last decades [1–7], and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is considered as a promising way to convert low-energy-density solar energy into high-density/green chemical energy [8–11]. Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4), an attracting low-cost photoanode matrix, has been widely applied for constructing efficient PEC water splitting system, and the suitable bandgap endows it with outstanding solar utilization and photovoltage for realizing unassisted overall pure water splitting [12,13]. The majority of BiVO4 failing to reach the theoretical photocurrent density (∼7.5 mA/cm2, AM 1.5 G) was mainly caused by its severe electron-hole recombination and poor charge transport [14–17]. One of promising strategies to address above issues is to fabricate heterostructure by integrating critical components into BiVO4 matrix, contributing to high solar utilization and reasonable barrier [18]. Most heterostructure based on different semiconductors could significantly improve the thermodynamic factors, but it hard to optimize the surface kinetic at the same time [19].

One simple approach for breaking the limitation of semiconductor/BiVO4 heterostructure is to implant noble metals on the surface of BiVO4 to form Schottky barriers, which can facilitate both photogenenrated charges separation and OER kinetic [20–22]. Among various noble metal materials, Ag is the relative low-cost noble metal for decorating semiconductor [23], and its localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect can also lead to the stronger light scattering and enhanced light absorption [24]. Therefore, Ag possesses the multi-functions of solar utilization, charges separation and OER active sites, which makes it widely applied in the configuration of PEC water splitting system based on BiVO4. For example, Li et al. have observed the enhanced PEC performance of BiVO4 decorated with Ag [25]. Jeong et al. further utilized LSPR to the developed BiVO4-Ag with efficient PEC water splitting activity, and the photocurrent density increased to 1.75 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE [26]. However, Ag exposed outside BiVO4 could be easily oxidized, causing the reduction of stability of Ag-BiVO4. Essential strategy to avoid the Ag oxidation behavior might be reached by decreasing holes accumulation and fabricating reduced chemical environment. Recent studies found that N-doped carbon medium can accelerate the charges transfer, and the lone pair electrons of N element also help for the formation of reducing environment [27]. To fabricate BiVO4-Ag intrigued with N-doped carbon is an acceptable ideal for further boosting the PEC performance of BiVO4 photoanode. Polydopamine (PDA), as an organic synthetic polymer, derived from self-polymerization of dopamine, which has a good affinity to semiconductor surfaces [28]. PDA takes a nature of reducibility and N resource precursor, benefiting for in-situ preparing Ag and N-doped carbon. So, PDA shows a potential in the fabrication of heterostructure based on BiVO4, Ag and N-doped carbon.

In this work, we present a route to evenly deposit Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) on BiVO4 surface with the assistance of PDA. BiVO4 with Ag and N-doped carbon (BiVO4-C/N-Ag) was successfully obtained after carbonization. The PEC performance of BiVO4-C/N-Ag was systematically studied under visible and UV light irradiation. We found that coupling BiVO4 with Ag NPs and N-doped carbon can facilitate charge transfer and stability, thereby significantly increase the photocurrent density and incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE). The improved PEC performance of BiVO4-C/N-Ag offers a strong potential for applications in solar energy conversion. On the basis of the experimental results, corresponding PEC mechanism of the BiVO4-C/N-Ag system has also been proposed.

All chemicals were analytical grade and utilized without further purification. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), potassium iodide (KI), nitric acid (HNO3), p-benzoquinone, ethanol, vanadyl acetylacetonate (VO(acac)2), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), silver nitrate (AgNO3), tris(hydroxymethyl) methyl aminomethane, hydrochloric acid, and dopamine hydrochloride were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical. Deionized water was used throughout all the experiment.

The BiVO4 was prepared according to the previously reported method [29]. Firstly, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (0.04 M) and KI (0.4 M) were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water (pH = 1.7 adjusted by HNO3). Then, 20 mL absolute ethanol containing 0.23 M p-benzoquinone was added into above solution and stirred. The obtained solution was transferred into the three-electrode system. The electrodeposition was carried out at room temperature (−0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl, 5 min). The prepared BiOI film was washed with deionized water, and 0.2 mL of DMSO solution containing 0.2 mol/L VO(acac)2 was dripped. Then, the sample was annealing in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for 2 h (2 °C/min). Afterwards, the V2O5 in sample was removed by 1 M NaOH solution, and BiVO4 photoanode were finally obtained.

First, 100 mL trs-hydrochloric acid buffer solution (pH = 8.5) was prepared with 0.6 g tris (hydroxymethyl) methyl aminomethane and 14.7 mL of 0.1 M dilute hydrochloric acid. Then, 0.2 g dopamine hydrochloride powder was dissolved in the above solution and mixed well. FTO glass coated with BiVO4 was dipped vertically into the above solution under gentle magnetic stirring. Then, the BiVO4 photoanodes were kept still for 1, 2, 3, and 4 h to conduct in-situ coating and labeled as BiVO4-n PDA. Finally, the BiVO4-n PDA was annealed at 450 °C in argon flow for 3 h. The resulting samples were labeled as BiVO4-1C/N, BiVO4-2C/N, BiVO4-3C/N, and BiVO4-4C/N, respectively.

The Ag NPs were deposited onto BiVO4-PDA via a simple dip/redox process [30]. In this experiment, BiVO4-PDA sample was dipped vertically into AgNO3 aqueous solution (50 mL) and kept still for 2 h. Then the treated sample was rinsed with deionized water and gently blown with nitrogen flow. After drying at 60 °C in vacuum for 12 h, the sample was annealed at 450 °C in argon flow for 3 h. Following this procedure, AgNO3 aqueous solutions with concentrations of 0.001, 0.01, 0.1 and 0.5 M were used to deposit various amounts of Ag. Finally, the samples were labeled as BiVO4-2C/N-0.001Ag, BiVO4-2C/N-0.01Ag, BiVO4-2C/N-0.1Ag and BiVO4-2C/N-0.5Ag, corresponding to the 0.001, 0.01, 0.1 and 0.5 M AgNO3 aqueous solution, respectively.

All the electrochemical and PEC measurements were performed in a three-electrode quartz cell (0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte) with a 1 cm2 window to facilitate illumination of the as-prepared samples, while Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrode and Pt electrode were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The illumination source was directed at the quartz PEC cell (radiation intensity: 100 mW/cm2). The photoelectrodes were irradiated through in-back side. The linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) test was carried out in a voltage window of −0.4 V to 1.2 V (vs Ag/AgCl) and a scanning rate of 50 mV/s using electrochemical workstation. The incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) tests was measured under monochromatic light (Newport 69920, 300 W xenon lamp) using a solar simulator by an external quantum efficiency measurement system (Newport/Oriel, QE-PV-SI) connected to the electrochemical workstation. Charge injection efficiency and charge separation efficiency were carried out in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution with/without H2O2. The photoelectrochemical impedance spectra (PEIS) (10−2 Hz to 106 Hz) and Mott−Schottky plots were measured using an electrochemical analyzer (Princeton, VersaSTAT 3).

The morphologies of samples were studied using a field emission scanning electronic microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800 25.0 kV 7.5 mm × 40.0 SE(U)). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) were carried out to characterize the structure using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin electron microscope (FEI Co.) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The structure and crystallinity of samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS VG, Physical Electrons Quantum 2000 Scanning Esca Microprob, Al Ka radiation) was carried out to confirm the surface chemical compositions and states of the samples. The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) was carried out with a UV2550 (Shimadzu, Japan).

Based on the structure nature of PDA, we reasonably realized the in-situ reduction of Ag+ ions by N-group, and the compact flexible matrix of PDA could further paly a confinement role for controlling the size of Ag nanoparticle. The thermal decomposition product from PDA further induced the formation of N-doped carbon. Moreover, the soft properties of PDA enabled N-doped carbon film to closed wrap on the surface of BiVO4. The detail procedures for BiVO4-C/N-Ag preparation has been shown in Scheme 1 .

The crystallographic structures of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD), which are presented in Fig. 1 . For bare BiVO4, the peaks at 29.3° and 31.1° can respond to diffractions from (1 2 1) and (0 4 0) planes of monoclinic scheelite BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 14-0688) [31]. Other diffraction peaks of BiVO4 can also be found in the XRD patterns, indicating that BiVO4 has been successfully synthesized. However, as for the XRD pattern of BiVO4-C/N and BiVO4-C/N-Ag, no diffraction peaks assigned to Ag and N-doped carbon film can be observed, resulting from the small crystalline size and low deposition content of Ag and N-doped carbon [32,33]. In order to further prove the existence of Ag and carbon component, the prepared samples were characterized by FESEM, HRTEM and XPS.

To verify the morphology and microstructure, the prepared samples are tested by FESEM and HRTEM. Fig. 2 (a–c) shows the typical FESEM images of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag, respectively. It can be clearly observed that the bare BiVO4 (Fig. 2a) is composed of loose wormlike structure with a smooth surface. After thermal decomposition, the obtained BiVO4-C/N (Fig. 2b) and BiVO4-C/N-Ag (Fig. 2c) present rough surface, indirectly revealing the surface modified operation. The thickness of BiVO4-C/N-Ag film is about 600 nm, according to the cross section FESEM image (Fig. 2d). Furthermore, the characteristic peaks of Bi, V, O, C, N, and Ag elements can be all checked through the selective EDS spectra of BiVO4-C/N-Ag sample (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). Elemental mapping images (Fig. S2, Supporting Information) well demonstrates that Ag and N-doped carbon have been evenly loaded on BiVO4. The Ag NPs can be obviously observed by TEM images (Fig. 2e), and the small size and acceptable dispersion can be attributed to the confinement effect of PDA matrix. As shown in the HR-TEM images of BiVO4-C/N-Ag, the thickness of N-doped carbon layer is about 8 nm, and the size of Ag nanoparticles is about 12 nm. When the microstructure image of sample was further magnified, two different sets of lattice planes can be clearly observed (Fig. 2f). The lattice fringes of 0.26 nm can be assigned to (2 0 0) plane of BiVO4, while the lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.23 nm corresponds to the (1 1 1) planes of Ag NPs. Therefore, the above results demonstrated that BiVO4 with selective N-doped carbon and Ag NPs modification (BiVO4-C/N-Ag) has been successfully synthesized.

To further confirm the elemental composition and chemical states of BiVO4-C/N-Ag, high-resolution XPS spectra studies of main elements were recorded. It can be found from the full XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S3, Supporting Information) that BiVO4-C/N-Ag contains Bi, V, O, C, N, and Ag elements. A typical high- resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4f is shown in Fig. 3 a. The Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 binding energies appears at 164.12 and 158.82 eV, confirming the presence of a Bi3+ species [34]. Meanwhile, V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2 peaks of V 2p (Fig. 3b) at a binding energy equal to 516.52 and 524.08 eV, referring to V5+ ions in the crystal lattice. Furthermore, three XPS peaks of O 1 s spectra are observed at the binding energies of 532.27, 530.86, and 529.65 eV (Fig. 3c), assigned to the adsorbed water, OH− specie, and the lattice sites (O2−) [35–37]. In Fig. 3d, the peak at 284.8 eV can be ascribed to C 1s, confirming presence of the C species [38]. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 3e) showed a clear peak at 399.84 eV, which represents that the N element has been successfully doped [39]. The Ag 3d spectra in Fig. 3f offers two obvious peaks at 367.84 (Ag 3d5/2) and 373.84 eV (Ag 3d3/2). The spin energy separation is 6.0 eV, revealing that Ag exists as metallic Ag species in the sample [40]. At the same time, XPS spectra of bare BiVO4 was also characterized (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). O1s core level spectra of both BiVO4 and BiVO4-C/N-Ag can be fitted into three peaks. The three peaks are assigned to O2−, OH− species, and adsorbed water, respectively. The area ratio of OH−/O2− species is higher in BiVO4-C/N-Ag than that of BiVO4. It is attributable to the decoration of Ag and N-doped carbon on the surface of BiVO4.

Fig. 4 a shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag. The BiVO4 exhibits a cliffy absorption edge at ∼500 nm, which corresponds to the intrinsic bandgap of BiVO4 [41]. For the BiVO4-C/N, modification of N-doped carbon can significantly extend the light absorption in the wavelength range from 200 to 800 nm, suggesting the enhanced light harvesting ability of N-doped carbon. The absorbance edges of BiVO4-C/N-Ag showed marked red shifts, which can be attributed to the localized LSPR effect of the loading Ag NPs [42], further confirming the existence of Ag NPs. The optical band gap energy of BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag were calculated by the Kubelka-Munk equation (Fig. 4b) [43], corresponding to 2.51, 2.46, and 2.42 eV, respectively. Thus, N-doped carbon and Ag NPs both play an important role in the solar utilization.

To examine the PEC performance of as-prepared photoelectrode, the linear-sweep-voltammetry (LSV) was measured under 100 mW/cm2 illumination (scan rate: 50 mV/s). The loading amount of N-doped carbon and Ag NPs has a great influence on the PEC performance (Fig. S5, S6 Supporting Information). Fig. 5 a presents a comparation of LSV curves among bare BiVO4, BiVO4-2C/N, and BiVO4-2C/N-0.01Ag. The bare BiVO4 shows a photocurrent density of 0.56 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, and BiVO4-2C/N achieves a photocurrent density of 1.19 mA/cm2 at the same potential. What's more, the BiVO4-2C/N-0.01Ag shows the highest photocurrent density (2.42 mA/cm2) at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which outperformed both BiVO4, and BiVO4-2C/N. The observably enlarged photocurrent density of BiVO4-2C/N-0.01Ag can be mainly attributed to the improvement of photoexcited electron-hole separation efficiency and interfacial electron transfer [44]. The incorporation of Ag NPs into BiVO4 leads to increased light absorption, which helps to generate more photoinduced electron–hole pairs, as well as the intense local electric fields formed around the Ag NPs, which enhances the separation of the electron–hole pairs. The BiVO4-2C/N-0.01Ag shows an apparent cathodic shift of onset potential (cathodic shift: ∼−0.1 V). This phenomenon can be attributed to the effect of N-doped carbon and Ag NPs, which greatly reduced electron–hole recombination and increase the kinetics of water oxidation [45]. However, there is very little cathodic shift for BiVO4-2C/N compared with bare BiVO4, and this result reveals that N-doped carbon could increase the solar absorption behavior but slightly work on the photovoltage and kinetics of photoanode. More importantly, the BiVO4-C/N-Ag photoanode shows the outstanding PEC performance compared with reported similar system, as shown in (Table 1, Supporting Information). The transient photocurrent responded to intermittent light (Fig. 5b) were tested at 1.23 V vs. RHE to further prove that the BiVO4-C/N-Ag possesses the best photo-responding performance in this system.

The photoelectrochemical impedance spectroscopy (PEIS) were performed to measure the charge transfer resistance of photoanodes (Fig. 5c). The smaller radius of the arc in PEIS curves usually indicates a smaller electron transfer resistance [46,47]. As expected, the PEIS Nyquist plots of BiVO4-C/N and BiVO4-C/N-Ag exhibits much smaller semicircles comparing with the bare BiVO4, which indicates that interfacial charge transfer of heterostructure has been enhanced. The equivalent circuit model of PEIS has been applied in the inset Fig. 5c. The Rct value of 1992.89 Ω for BiVO4-C/N-Ag photoanode is smaller than that of BiVO4-C/N (2173.46 Ω) and BiVO4 (2293.29 Ω). Smaller Rct values correspond to more favorable charge transport across the electrode/electrolyte interface, indicating that BiVO4-C/N-Ag photoanode shows superior charge transport property. The photo-generated electrons inside BiVO4 would favorably transfer across the BiVO4-C/N-Ag interface to Ag NPs under the influence of the Schottky barrier until reach the charge equilibrium. The surface of Ag NPs obtains excessive negative charge and BiVO4 shows excessive positive charge, then BiVO4 is induced to produce energy band bending. The energy band bending can promote the separation of electron-hole. To evaluate the wavelength-dependent light harvesting efficiency of as-prepared photoanodes, the IPCE measurement was performed under monochromatic light illumination from 350 to 580 nm at 1.23 V vs. RHE (Fig. 5d), according to the formula [48]: IPCE = 1240I pH/(λJ light). Where I pH is the measured photocurrent density (mA/cm2), λ is the wavelength of incident light, and J light is the power intensity (mW/cm2). The IPCE value of BiVO4-C/N-Ag is higher than that of bare BiVO4 and BiVO4-C/N. In addition, the highest IPCE value of BiVO4-C/N-Ag can reach up to 30% (400 nm), which is 7.5-fold higher than that of bare BiVO4. Thus, the modification of N-doped carbon layer and Ag NPs significantly extend the light harvesting ability and charge transfer of sample [49], thereby improving PEC water splitting performance.

Mott-Schottky plots measurement was performed to characterize the flat band potential (E fb) and charge carrier density (N d) of the as-prepared photoanodes. As shown in Fig. 6 a–c, the BiVO4, BiVO4-C/N, and BiVO4-C/N-Ag show a linear region with a positive slope, which is typical of n-type BiVO4 semiconductor characteristic. The E fb is measured by extrapolating each plot to the x-axis to get the intercept value. The N d can be calculated by following formula [50]:
N d
=
(
2 / e
ε 0
ε
)
[
d (
1 /
C 2
) / d V
]
- 1
=
(
2 / e
ε 0
ε
)
[
1 / s l o p e
]

E fb is 0.1 V vs. RHE for BiVO4, 0.14 V vs. RHE for BiVO4-C/N, and 0.2 V vs. RHE for BiVO4-C/N-Ag, and the positive shift indicates an increase in the bending of band edges, thereby enhancing the charge separation efficiency. Furthermore, the BiVO4-C/N-Ag showed a smaller slope value than that of bare BiVO4 and BiVO4-C/N, indicating that a higher carrier density has been obtained in heterostructure. It means that modification of the N-doped carbon layer and Ag NPs can significantly enhance the electrical conductance and charge migration.

To in-depth reveal the role of N-doped carbon and Ag NPs on the PEC performances, the detections of the charge injection efficiency (η inj) and charge separation efficiency (η sep) were carried out with the assistance of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as a hole scavenger [28]. The η inj and η sep can be obtained according to the formula [51]: η inj = J photocurrent/J H2O2 and η sep = J H2O2/J max, where J H2O2 is the photocurrent density in the presence of H2O2, J max is the theoretical maximum photocurrent. As shown in Fig. 6d, an impressive enhancement on η inj can be observed for BiVO4 coupled with N doped carbon and Ag NPs, which is improved from 27.88% for BiVO4 to 53.64% for BiVO4-C/N, and 88.59% for BiVO4-C/N-Ag at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The promoted η inj value is derived from the high catalytic behavior of the N-doped carbon and Ag NPs [52], which can be judged by the obvious negative shift of the LSV curves onset potential. In addition, the η sep was also detected that coupling with the N-doped carbon and Ag NPs can also result in a promotion of the charge separation in the photoanode bulk (Fig. 6e), which was increased from 23.86% (BiVO4) to 25.63% (BiVO4-C/N) and 29.75% (BiVO4-C/N-Ag) at 1.23 V vs. RHE. These results reveal that the positive outcomes of the N-doped carbon and Ag NPs can not only benefit for charge separation in the photoanode bulk, but also the water oxidation kinetics promotion on photoanode surface [53,54].

Taking account of the potential of this PEC system in industrial applications, the long-term stability of the photoelectrode is one of the important parameters [55]. As shown in the Fig. 6f, BiVO4-C/N-Ag can maintain the 92.8% photocurrent density of the initial value over 5 h under light irradiation, indicating its outstanding photoelectric durability for PEC water splitting system. In order to in-depth confirm the station of Ag during PEC process, the XPS spectra of BiVO4-C/N-Ag was performed after stability measurement and labeled as BiVO4-C/N-Ag (5 h) (Fig. S7b, Supporting Information). Compared with BiVO4-C/N-Ag, it is found that no obvious peaks of Ag+ appeared in the XPS spectra of BiVO4-C/N-Ag (5 h), revealing that N-doped carbon could effectively protect Ag from oxidation, and this phenomena is consistent with stability measurement. Therefore, the BiVO4-C/N-Ag photoanode shows great potential in future PEC industrial applications.

Based on the above experimental results, the possible mechanism of the charge transfer process of PEC water oxidation over the BiVO4-C/N-Ag has been further proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . Firstly, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs were generated by the absorption of photons with energy equal to or greater than its band-gap [56,57]. In this process, the N-doped carbon can increase the light harvesting range of BiVO4 and accelerated interfacial charge transfer. On the other hand, Ag and BiVO4 possess the different Fermi levels, and Ag's work function are higher than BiVO4′s work function. When the Ag NPs and BiVO4 are in contact, photoexcited electrons migrate from BiVO4 to Ag until the Fermi levels of Ag and BiVO4 match, forming a space charge layer at the metal/semiconductor interface. The surface of Ag NPs obtains excessive negative charge and BiVO4 shows excessive positive charge, then BiVO4 is induced to produce energy band bending, and Schottky barrier is formed [58,59]. The energy band bending and space charge layer can promote the separation of electron-hole and the diffusion of charge carriers. The Schottky barrier at the interface between Ag NPs and BiVO4 will effectively act as an electron trap to prevent electron-hole recombination in photoelectrochemical processes. Finally, the electrons and holes are transferred to FTO substrates and semiconductor/electrolyte interface through N-doped carbon medium, respectively. Therefore, the integration photoanode built from BiVO4, Ag and N-doped carbon contributed to the significant enhancement of PEC water splitting performance.

In summary, we have successfully prepared a novel BiVO4-C/N-Ag via in-situ dip/redox capture for the first time. The BiVO4-C/N-Ag exhibits the excellent light harvesting due to the N-doped carbon and LSPR effect. The photocurrent of BiVO4-C/N-Ag boosts to 2.42 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is 4.32-fold higher than that of the bare BiVO4 photoanode. The BiVO4-C/N-Ag also exhibits a maximum IPCE value of 30% at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is ∼7.5 times to that of the bare BiVO4. Besides, the outstanding photoelectric durability for BiVO4-C/N-Ag was achieved in comparison with the bare BiVO4. The enhanced PEC activity can be due to the excellent light trapping capability, enhanced charge transport process as well as efficient photoexcited electron-hole separation. Therefore, these efficient performances make BiVO4-C/N-Ag possess great potential in solving the dilemma of current investigations on PEC water splitting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21671083, 21401082 and 21522603), Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2017M621644 and 2018T110454), Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilizations (RERU2018011), Jiangsu Provincial Six Talent Peaks (XCL-019), Jiangsu Province Natural Science Foundation (BK20170526) and Youth Backbone Teacher Training Engineering of Jiangsu University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.10.066.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
